blocks:
Ayahs

سَبَّحَ لِلَّهِ مَا فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَمَا فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ ۖ وَهُوَ ٱلْعَزِيزُ ٱلْحَكِيمُ (١)
Whatever there is in the heavens and whatever there is in the earth glorifies Allah, and He is the All-mighty, the All-wise. (1)
هُوَ ٱلَّذِىٓ أَخْرَجَ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ مِنْ أَهْلِ ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ مِن دِيَـٰرِهِمْ لِأَوَّلِ ٱلْحَشْرِ ۚ مَا ظَنَنتُمْ أَن يَخْرُجُوا۟ ۖ وَظَنُّوٓا۟ أَنَّهُم مَّانِعَتُهُمْ حُصُونُهُم مِّنَ ٱللَّهِ فَأَتَىٰهُمُ ٱللَّهُ مِنْ حَيْثُ لَمْ يَحْتَسِبُوا۟ ۖ وَقَذَفَ فِى قُلُوبِهِمُ ٱلرُّعْبَ ۚ يُخْرِبُونَ بُيُوتَهُم بِأَيْدِيهِمْ وَأَيْدِى ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ فَٱعْتَبِرُوا۟ يَـٰٓأُو۟لِى ٱلْأَبْصَـٰرِ (٢)
It is He who expelled the faithless belonging to the People of the Book from their homes at the outset of [their] en masse banishment. You did not think that they would go out, and they thought their fortresses would protect them from Allah. But Allah came at them from whence they did not suppose and He cast terror into their hearts. They demolish their houses with their own hands and the hands of the faithful. So take lesson, O you who have insight! (2)
وَلَوْلَآ أَن كَتَبَ ٱللَّهُ عَلَيْهِمُ ٱلْجَلَآءَ لَعَذَّبَهُمْ فِى ٱلدُّنْيَا ۖ وَلَهُمْ فِى ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ عَذَابُ ٱلنَّارِ (٣)
If Allah had not ordained banishment for them, He would have surely punished them in this world, and in the Hereafter, there is the punishment of the Fire for them. (3)
ذَٰلِكَ بِأَنَّهُمْ شَآقُّوا۟ ٱللَّهَ وَرَسُولَهُۥ ۖ وَمَن يُشَآقِّ ٱللَّهَ فَإِنَّ ٱللَّهَ شَدِيدُ ٱلْعِقَابِ (٤)
That is because they defied Allah and His Apostle; and whoever defies Allah, Allah is indeed severe in retribution. (4)
مَا قَطَعْتُم مِّن لِّينَةٍ أَوْ تَرَكْتُمُوهَا قَآئِمَةً عَلَىٰٓ أُصُولِهَا فَبِإِذْنِ ٱللَّهِ وَلِيُخْزِىَ ٱلْفَـٰسِقِينَ (٥)
Whatever palm trees you cut down or left standing on their roots, it was by Allah’s will and in order that He may disgrace the transgressors. (5)
وَمَآ أَفَآءَ ٱللَّهُ عَلَىٰ رَسُولِهِۦ مِنْهُمْ فَمَآ أَوْجَفْتُمْ عَلَيْهِ مِنْ خَيْلٍ وَلَا رِكَابٍ وَلَـٰكِنَّ ٱللَّهَ يُسَلِّطُ رُسُلَهُۥ عَلَىٰ مَن يَشَآءُ ۚ وَٱللَّهُ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ قَدِيرٌ (٦)
The spoils that Allah gave to His Apostle from them, you did not spur any horse for its sake, nor any riding camel, but Allah makes His apostles prevail over whomever He wishes, and Allah has power over all things. (6)
مَّآ أَفَآءَ ٱللَّهُ عَلَىٰ رَسُولِهِۦ مِنْ أَهْلِ ٱلْقُرَىٰ فَلِلَّهِ وَلِلرَّسُولِ وَلِذِى ٱلْقُرْبَىٰ وَٱلْيَتَـٰمَىٰ وَٱلْمَسَـٰكِينِ وَٱبْنِ ٱلسَّبِيلِ كَىْ لَا يَكُونَ دُولَةًۢ بَيْنَ ٱلْأَغْنِيَآءِ مِنكُمْ ۚ وَمَآ ءَاتَىٰكُمُ ٱلرَّسُولُ فَخُذُوهُ وَمَا نَهَىٰكُمْ عَنْهُ فَٱنتَهُوا۟ ۚ وَٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ ۖ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ شَدِيدُ ٱلْعِقَابِ (٧)
The spoils that Allah gave to His Apostle from the people of the townships, are for Allah and the Apostle, the relatives and the orphans, the needy and the traveller, so that they do not circulate among the rich among you. Take whatever the Apostle gives you, and refrain from whatever he forbids you, and be wary of Allah. Indeed Allah is severe in retribution. (7)
لِلْفُقَرَآءِ ٱلْمُهَـٰجِرِينَ ٱلَّذِينَ أُخْرِجُوا۟ مِن دِيَـٰرِهِمْ وَأَمْوَٰلِهِمْ يَبْتَغُونَ فَضْلًا مِّنَ ٱللَّهِ وَرِضْوَٰنًا وَيَنصُرُونَ ٱللَّهَ وَرَسُولَهُۥٓ ۚ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ هُمُ ٱلصَّـٰدِقُونَ (٨)
[They are also] for the poor Emigrants who have been expelled from their homes and [wrested of] their possessions, who seek grace from Allah and [His] pleasure and help Allah and His Apostle. It is they who are the truthful. (8)
وَٱلَّذِينَ تَبَوَّءُو ٱلدَّارَ وَٱلْإِيمَـٰنَ مِن قَبْلِهِمْ يُحِبُّونَ مَنْ هَاجَرَ إِلَيْهِمْ وَلَا يَجِدُونَ فِى صُدُورِهِمْ حَاجَةً مِّمَّآ أُوتُوا۟ وَيُؤْثِرُونَ عَلَىٰٓ أَنفُسِهِمْ وَلَوْ كَانَ بِهِمْ خَصَاصَةٌ ۚ وَمَن يُوقَ شُحَّ نَفْسِهِۦ فَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ هُمُ ٱلْمُفْلِحُونَ (٩)
[They are as well] for those who were settled in the land and [abided] in faith before them, who love those who migrate toward them, and do not find in their breasts any privation for that which is given to them, but prefer [the Immigrants] to themselves, though poverty be their own lot. And those who are saved from their own greed—it is they who are the felicitous. (9)
وَٱلَّذِينَ جَآءُو مِنۢ بَعْدِهِمْ يَقُولُونَ رَبَّنَا ٱغْفِرْ لَنَا وَلِإِخْوَٰنِنَا ٱلَّذِينَ سَبَقُونَا بِٱلْإِيمَـٰنِ وَلَا تَجْعَلْ فِى قُلُوبِنَا غِلًّا لِّلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ رَبَّنَآ إِنَّكَ رَءُوفٌ رَّحِيمٌ (١٠)
And [also for] those who came in after them, who say, ‘Our Lord, forgive us and our brethren who were our forerunners in the faith, and do not put any rancour in our hearts toward the faithful. Our Lord, You are indeed most kind and merciful.’ (10)

(Commentary)

The Surah refers to the story of the expulsion of the Banu al-Nadir from among the Jews when they violated the covenant between them and the Muslims. It also points to the promise of the hypocrites to support them and remain with them, followed by their betrayal, and the subsequent rulings regarding their spoils (الفيء) — property taken from the enemy without a formal war or military engagement.

Among the most luminous verses in this Surah are the seven verses at its conclusion, in which Allah, the Glorified, commands His servants to prepare for His meeting through the path of self-supervision (المراقبة) — the constant awareness of Allah's presence — and self-accounting (المحاسبة) — the systematic auditing of one's deeds. He mentions the greatness of His speech and the majesty of its status by describing the greatness of the Speaker, Exalted is He as a Speaker (عز من قائل), through His Beautiful Names (الأسماء الحسنى) and Sublime Attributes (الصفات العليا). The Surah is Medinan, as evidenced by the context of its verses.

His saying, the Exalted: سَبَّحَ لِلَّهِ ما فِي السَّماواتِ وَما فِي الْأَرْضِ وَهُوَ الْعَزِيزُ الْحَكِيمُ "Whatever is in the heavens and whatever is on the earth glorifies Allah, and He is the All-mighty, the All-wise" (59:1). This opening corresponds to what appears at the end of the Surah in His saying: يُسَبِّحُ لَهُ ما فِي السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ وَهُوَ الْعَزِيزُ الْحَكِيمُ "Whatever is in the heavens and the earth glorifies Him, and He is the All-mighty, the All-wise" (59:24).

The Surah opens with glorification (التنزيه) because of the references within it to the treachery of the Jews and their violation of the covenant, followed by the hypocrites' deceptive promise of victory to them—resembling those shortly before them who tasted the evil consequences of their conduct. In view of the bitter consequences Allah made them taste for their plotting, and because this occurred according to what wisdom (الحكمة) and interest (المصلحة) necessitated, He concluded the verse with His saying: وَهُوَ الْعَزِيزُ الْحَكِيمُ "and He is the All-mighty, the All-wise."

His saying, the Exalted: هُوَ الَّذِي أَخْرَجَ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا مِنْ أَهْلِ الْكِتابِ مِنْ دِيارِهِمْ لِأَوَّلِ الْحَشْرِ "It is He who expelled the faithless from among the People of the Book from their homes at the first banishment" (59:2). This reinforces what was mentioned in the previous verse regarding His transcendence, might, and wisdom. The "expulsion of the faithless from among the People of the Book" refers to the banishment of the Banu al-Nadir, a Jewish tribe who lived outside Medina. There was a covenant between them and the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله that they would be neither for him nor against him. They then violated the covenant, so the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله expelled them. Their story will follow in the upcoming "Discussion on Traditions," Allah willing.

The term banishment (الحشر) refers to the removal of a group with force and disturbance. The phrase "at the first banishment" (لِأَوَّلِ الْحَشْرِ) is an instance of an adjective being added to the noun it describes. The preposition lam (اللام) here carries the meaning of "in" (في), as in His saying: أَقِمِ الصَّلاةَ لِدُلُوكِ الشَّمْسِ "Maintain the prayer from the sun's decline" (17:78).

The meaning is: Allah is the One who expelled the Banu al-Nadir from among the Jews from their homes during the first instance of their expulsion from the Arabian Peninsula.

Then, He points to the significance of their expulsion by saying: ما ظَنَنْتُمْ أَنْ يَخْرُجُوا "You did not think that they would leave" because of the strength, intensity, and fortification you observed in them. وَظَنُّوا أَنَّهُمْ مانِعَتُهُمْ حُصُونُهُمْ مِنَ اللهِ "and they thought their fortresses would protect them from Allah." That is, they thought Allah would not overcome them while they were fortified within them. He regarded their fortresses—according to their own assumption—as being a protection "from Allah" rather than from the Muslims, because their expulsion from them was attributed to Him, the Exalted, in the preceding verse, as was the casting of terror into their hearts at the end of the verse. The phrasing indicates that they possessed multiple fortresses.

He then mentions the fallacy of their assumption and the error of their claim by saying: فَأَتاهُمُ اللهُ مِنْ حَيْثُ لَمْ يَحْتَسِبُوا "But Allah came at them from whence they did not expect." This refers to the penetration of His will among them, not through a path they anticipated—which was the path of fortresses and gates—but through their inner selves, which is the path of the hearts. وَقَذَفَ فِي قُلُوبِهِمُ الرُّعْبَ "and He cast terror into their hearts." Terror (الرعب) is the fear that fills the heart. يُخْرِبُونَ بُيُوتَهُمْ بِأَيْدِيهِمْ "They were razing their homes with their own hands" so that they would not fall into the hands of the believers after their departure. This is an instance of the power of His authority over them, as He executed what He willed through their own hands. وَأَيْدِي الْمُؤْمِنِينَ "and the hands of the believers" as He commanded them to do so and granted them success in obeying His command and executing His will. فَاْعَتَبِرُوا "So take a lesson" and take heed from the admonition. يا أُولِي الْأَبْصارِ "O you who have vision" regarding what you witness of the handiwork of the All-mighty, the All-wise, against them in response to their opposition to Him and His Messenger.

It is said that they were destroying the houses to flee, while the believers were destroying them to reach them. It is also said that "razing the homes" refers to the disruption of their way of life; they destroyed their homes with their own hands by violating the peace treaty, and by the hands of the believers by inciting them to fight. However, the apparent meaning of His saying, "They were razing their homes..." is that it serves as an explanation for His saying, "But Allah came at them from whence they did not expect..." in terms of its effect; thus, it is subsequent to the violation of the peace treaty.

His saying, the Exalted: وَلَوْ لا أَنْ كَتَبَ اللهُ عَلَيْهِمُ الْجَلاءَ لَعَذَّبَهُمْ فِي الدُّنْيا وَلَهُمْ فِي الْآخِرَةِ عَذَابُ النَّارِ "Had Allah not ordained banishment for them, He would have surely punished them in this world, and for them there is the punishment of the Fire in the Hereafter" (59:3). Banishment (الجلاء) is the leaving of one's homeland, and the "ordaining" (كتابة) of banishment upon them is His decree (قضاؤه) regarding them. The "punishment in this world" refers to the punishment of extermination, or killing and captivity.

The meaning is: Had Allah not decreed for them to leave their homes and abandon their homeland, He would have punished them in this world with the punishment of extermination, or killing and captivity, as He did with the Banu Qurayzah; and for them in the Hereafter is the punishment of the Fire.

His saying, the Exalted: ذلِكَ بِأَنَّهُمْ شَاقُّوا اللهَ وَرَسُولَهُ وَمَنْ يُشَاقِّ اللهَ فَإِنَّ اللهَ شَدِيدُ الْعِقابِ "That is because they defied Allah and His Apostle, and whoever defies Allah, then Allah is indeed severe in retribution" (59:4). Defiance (المشاقة) is opposition rooted in stubbornness. The word "that" (ذلِكَ) refers to the mentioned expulsion and their deserving of punishment had banishment not been ordained for them. In specifying their defiance against Allah in the phrase "and whoever defies Allah" after generalizing it to Allah and His Messenger in the phrase "they defied Allah and His Apostle," there is an allusion that defying the Messenger is defying Allah. The rest is clear.

His saying, the Exalted: ما قَطَعْتُمْ مِنْ لِينَةٍ أَوْ تَرَكْتُمُوها قائِمَةً عَلى أُصُولِها فَبِإِذْنِ اللهِ وَلِيُخْزِيَ الْفاسِقِينَ "Whatever palm tree you cut down or left standing on its roots, it was by Allah’s leave, and that He may disgrace the transgressors" (59:5). Al-Raghib mentioned that the linah (اللينة) is the tender palm tree, without specializing it to one type over another. They narrated that the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله ordered the cutting of their palm trees. When some were cut, they called out to him: "O Muhammad, you used to forbid corruption on earth; why then are the palm trees being cut?" Then the verse was revealed. The response to their statement was that whatever palm tree they cut or left standing on its roots was by Allah's leave. Allah has true goals and profound wisdom in this ruling, one of which is the disgracing of the transgressors (الفاسقين), namely the Banu al-Nadir.

Thus, in His saying, "and that He may disgrace the transgressors," the lam (اللام) denotes causality and is coordinated with a deleted phrase. The estimation is: "The cutting and the leaving were by Allah's leave so that He might do such-and-after and that He may disgrace the transgressors." It is like His saying: وَكَذلِكَ نُرِي إِبْراهِيمَ مَلَكُوتَ السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ وَلِيَكُونَ مِنَ الْمُوقِنِينَ "Thus did We show Abraham the dominion of the heavens and the earth, that he might be of those who possess certitude" (6:75).

His saying, the Exalted: وَما أَفاءَ اللهُ عَلى رَسُولِهِ مِنْهُمْ فَما أَوْجَفْتُمْ عَلَيْهِ مِنْ خَيْلٍ وَلا رِكابٍ وَلكِنَّ اللهَ يُسَلِّطُ رُسُلَهُ عَلى مَنْ يَشاءُ "The spoils that Allah gave to His Apostle from them, you did not spur any horse or camel for their sake, but Allah makes His apostles prevail over whomever He wishes..." (59:6). The term ifa'ah (الإفاءة) means returning, derived from fay' (الفيء) meaning "to return." The pronoun "from them" refers to the Banu al-Nadir, and the meaning refers to their wealth.

Spurring (إيجاف) a beast means making it move with agitation and speed. Khayl (الخيل) refers to horses, and rikab (الركاب) refers to camels. The phrase "any horse or camel" is the object of "you did not spur," and the min (مِنْ) is extra for the sake of exhaustiveness (الاستغراق).

The meaning is: That which Allah returned to His Messenger from the wealth of the Banu al-Nadir—He specified it for him and made him its sole owner. You did not drive any horse or camel by riding to obtain it such that you would have a right in it; rather, you walked to their fortresses on foot due to their proximity to Medina. But Allah makes His messengers prevail over whomever He wishes, and Allah has power over all things. He made the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله prevail over the Banu al-Nadir, so their spoils belong to him to do with as he wishes.

His saying, the Exalted: ما أَفاءَ اللهُ عَلى رَسُولِهِ مِنْ أَهْلِ الْقُرى فَلِلَّهِ وَلِلرَّسُولِ وَلِذِي الْقُرْبى وَالْيَتامى وَالْمَساكِينِ وَابْنِ السَّبِيلِ "The spoils that Allah gave to His Apostle from the people of the townships, are for Allah and the Apostle, the relatives and the orphans, the needy and the traveller..." (59:7). Its apparent meaning is that it is an explanation of the avenues for spending the spoils mentioned in the previous verse, while generalizing the spoils to include those from the people of the townships, whether from the Banu al-Nadir or others.

His saying, "are for Allah and the Apostle," means that a portion of it is specified for Allah—meaning it is to be spent and expended in the way of Allah as the Messenger sees fit—and a portion is what the Messenger takes for himself. One should not heed the claim of those who say that the mention of the Exalted alongside the shareholders is merely for seeking blessings (التبرك).

His saying, "and the relatives (لِذِي الْقُرْبى)..." refers to the relatives of the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله. There is no sense in interpreting it as the relatives of the believers in general, as is evident. The "orphans" refers to the poor among them, as suggested by the context. The term is mentioned individually and placed before "the needy" (الْمَساكِينِ), despite being included under that category, out of special concern for the affairs of orphans.

It has been narrated from the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt عليه‌السلام that "the relatives" refers to the Ahl al-Bayt, and the orphans, the needy, and the traveller are from among them.

His saying: كَيْ لا يَكُونَ دُولَةً بَيْنَ الْأَغْنِياءِ مِنْكُمْ "so that it may not circulate [only] among the rich among you." That is, We have ruled regarding the spoils as We have ruled so that it does not become a dulah (دولة) among the rich among you. A dulah is that which is circulated among people and passes from hand to hand.

His saying: وَما آتاكُمُ الرَّسُولُ فَخُذُوهُ وَما نَهاكُمْ عَنْهُ فَانْتَهُوا "Take whatever the Apostle gives you, and relinquish whatever he forbids you." That is, whatever the Messenger gives you from the spoils, take it—as he gave from it to the Emigrants (المهاجرين) and a group of the Helpers (الأنصار)—and whatever he forbids you from and denies you, desist and do not seek it. This contains an allusion that they had asked the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله to divide the spoils among them all, so He returned it to His Prophet, designated its avenues of expenditure as mentioned in the verse, and granted the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله the right to spend it therein as he saw fit.

The verse, setting aside the context, is general and encompasses everything the Prophet brings of rulings, whether he commands it or forbids it.

His saying: وَاتَّقُوا اللهَ إِنَّ اللهَ شَدِيدُ الْعِقابِ "And be wary of Allah. Indeed Allah is severe in retribution." This is a warning to them against opposing the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, reinforcing His saying: "Take whatever the Apostle gives you..."

His saying, the Exalted: لِلْفُقَراءِ الْمُهاجِرِينَ الَّذِينَ أُخْرِجُوا مِنْ دِيارِهِمْ وَأَمْوالِهِمْ يَبْتَغُونَ فَضْلاً مِنَ اللهِ وَرِضْواناً "[They are] for the needy Emigrants who have been expelled from their homes and possessions, seeking Allah’s grace and [His] pleasure..." (59:8). It is said that His saying, "for the needy," is a substitute (بدل) for His saying, "for the relatives" and what follows it, and that the mention of Allah was merely for seeking blessings; thus, the spoils would be exclusive to the Messenger and the needy among the Emigrants. A narration has been recorded that the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله divided the spoils of the Banu al-Nadir among the Emigrants and did not give anything to the Helpers except for two or three of their needy men.

It is also said that it is a substitute for "the orphans, the needy, and the traveller," so the shareholders would be the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, the relatives—both their wealthy and their poor—and the needy among the Emigrants, including their orphans, their needy, and their travellers. Perhaps this is the intent of those who said that His saying, "for the needy Emigrants," is an explanation of "the needy" in the previous verse.

What is more appropriate to what was previously quoted from the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt عليه‌السلام is that His saying, "for the needy Emigrants..." is an explanation of an instance (مصداق) for spending in the way of Allah, which was alluded to by His saying, "for Allah." This is not to say that the needy Emigrants are one of the fixed shareholders of the spoils, but rather that spending on them and giving to them constitutes spending in the way of Allah.

The summary of the meaning based on this is: Allah, the Glorified, granted the spoils and returned them to the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, so he may dispose of them as he wishes. Then He guided him to the avenues of its expenditure, which are the way of Allah, the Messenger, the relatives, and their orphans, their needy, and their travellers. Then He pointed to an instance of spending in the "way" (السبيل), or some of its instances, which are the needy Emigrants, etc., upon whom the Messenger spends as he sees fit.

Based on this, one should interpret the reports that the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله divided the spoils of the Banu al-Nadir among the Emigrants and gave nothing to the Helpers except for three of their needy: Abu Dujanah Simak ibn Kharashah, Sahl ibn Hunayf, and al-Harith ibn al-Simmah. He spent on them insofar as it was spending in the way of Allah, not because they were fixed shareholders in the spoils.

In any case, His saying: "for the needy Emigrants who have been expelled from their homes and possessions" refers to those Muslims who emigrated from Mecca to Medina before the Conquest. They were the ones whom the faithless of Mecca forced to leave, so they abandoned their homes and wealth and emigrated to the city of the Messenger.

His saying: "seeking Allah’s grace and [His] pleasure." Grace (الفضل) refers to provision (الرزق); that is, they seek provision from Allah in this world and pleasure in the Hereafter.

His saying: "and help Allah and His Apostle." That is, they help Him and His Messenger with their wealth and their lives. His saying: "It is they who are the truthful" is a confirmation of their truthfulness in their affair while they possess these attributes.

His saying, the Exalted: وَالَّذِينَ تَبَوَّؤُا الدَّارَ وَالْإِيمانَ مِنْ قَبْلِهِمْ يُحِبُّونَ مَنْ هاجَرَ إِلَيْهِمْ "[They are also for] those who were settled in the land and [abided] in faith before them, who love those who migrate toward them..." (59:9). It is said that this is a new sentence (استئناف) intended to praise the Helpers to comfort their hearts since they were not included in the spoils. "Those who were settled" (وَالَّذِينَ تَبَوَّؤُا)—referring to the Helpers—is the subject (مبتدأ), and its predicate (خبر) is "who love..." etc. The "settling of the home" (تبوي الدار), which is its cultivation, refers metaphorically to the building of a religious society where the believers could take refuge. "Faith" (الْإِيمانَ) is coordinated with "the land" (الدَّارَ). The "settling of faith" and its cultivation means removing its deficiencies in terms of action, such that one is able to act upon the acts of obedience and proximity it calls for without restriction or prevention, as was the case in Mecca.

It is also possible that "faith" is coordinated with "settled" (تبوؤا) while the governing verb has been deleted, the estimation being: "and they chose faith."

It is also said that His saying, "and those who were settled..." is coordinated with His saying, "the Emigrants." According to this, the Helpers would share the spoils with the Emigrants. The objection that the narrated reports state the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله divided it among the Emigrants and gave nothing to the Helpers except for three of their needy is refuted by the fact that the narration is an evidence for coordination rather than a new sentence; for if it were not permissible to give to the Helpers, it would not be permissible for three nor for one. Thus, giving to some of them is proof of their participation, except that since the matter was referred to the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, he had the right to spend it as he wished, so he preferred to divide it among them in that manner.

What is more appropriate to what was previously mentioned—that "for the needy" is an explanation of the instances of the "way" (السبيل) share—is the coordination of "and those who were settled..." as well as the subsequent "and those who came after them" with His saying, "the Emigrants," rather than it being a new sentence.

Indeed, the report of his صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله giving to the three supports this very view; for if the shareholders were only the needy Emigrants, he would not have given to the Helpers, not even to three of them. And if the needy among the Helpers had a share like the Emigrants—and the apparent meaning of the verse is that a group of them were needy and in want, which history supports—he would have given to others among the needy Helpers just as he gave to the needy Emigrants and included them all.

Thus, in His saying: "and those who were settled in the land and [abided] in faith before them," the pronoun "before them" refers to the Emigrants, meaning before their arrival and their emigration to Medina.

His saying: "who love those who migrate toward them" means they love those who emigrated to them because of their emigration from the land of faithlessness to the land of faith and the society of Muslims.

His saying: "and do not find in their breasts any need for what they have been given." The pronouns in "find" and "their breasts" refer to the Helpers, and the pronoun in "they have been given" refers to the Emigrants. "Need" (حاجة) refers to that which is needed. The min (مِنْ) is partitive (تبعيضية), or it is said to be explanatory (بيانية). The meaning is: nothing of what was given to the Emigrants occurs to their minds, so their souls do not feel constrained by the division of the spoils among the Emigrants to their exclusion, nor do they feel envy.

It is also said that "need" refers to that which need leads to, which is resentment.

His saying: "and prefer [them] to themselves, though they may be in need." To prefer (إيثار) something is to choose it and put it before something else. Khasasah (الخصاصة) is poverty and need. Al-Raghib said: "The khasas of a house are its gaps, and poverty that has not been filled is expressed as khasasah, just as it is expressed as khallah (void)."

The meaning is: they put the Emigrants before themselves even if they are in poverty and need. This quality is more abundant and profound in their praise than the previous quality. The speech is in the sense of a transition (إضراب), as if it were said: they do not look longingly at what is in the hands of the Emigrants; rather, they put them before themselves regarding what is in their own hands, despite their own poverty and need.

His saying: "and those who are saved from their own greed—it is they who are the felicitous." Al-Raghib said: "Shuhh (الشح) is stinginess combined with greed in what is customary." The word yuqa (يوق) is a passive present tense verb from wiqayah (الوقاية) meaning protection. The meaning is: whoever is protected—that is, Allah protects him—from the narrowness of his soul regarding the giving of the wealth in his hand or regarding wealth falling into the hand of another, then it is they who are the felicitous.

His saying, the Exalted: وَالَّذِينَ جاؤُ مِنْ بَعْدِهِمْ يَقُولُونَ رَبَّنَا اغْفِرْ لَنا وَلِإِخْوانِنَا الَّذِينَ سَبَقُونا بِالْإِيمانِ "And [also] those who came after them, who say, ‘Our Lord, forgive us and our brethren who preceded us in faith...’" (59:10). This is either a new sentence or coordinated, similar to what was mentioned regarding His saying: "and those who were settled in the land and [abided] in faith before them, who love..." If it is a new sentence, the relative pronoun is the subject and its predicate is His saying: "who say, ‘Our Lord...’"

The "coming after" the Emigrants and the Helpers refers to their faith after the cessation of emigration following the Conquest. It is also said to mean that they succeeded them.

Their saying: "Our Lord, forgive us and our brethren who preceded us in faith" is a prayer for forgiveness for themselves and for the preceding believers. In their expression "our brethren," there is an allusion that they consider them as part of themselves, as Allah the Exalted said: بَعْضُكُمْ مِنْ بَعْضٍ "you are all one from another" (4:25). Thus, they love them as they love themselves and love for them what they love for themselves.

Therefore, they followed it with their saying: وَلا تَجْعَلْ فِي قُلُوبِنا غِلًّا لِلَّذِينَ آمَنُوا رَبَّنا إِنَّكَ رَؤُفٌ رَحِيمٌ "and do not put any rancour in our hearts toward the faithful. Our Lord, You are indeed most kind and merciful." They asked that Allah not place any rancour (غلا) in their hearts toward those who believe; ghill is enmity.

In His saying, "toward the faithful," there is a generalization to all believers, both from among themselves and those who preceded them, and an allusion that they have no desire except for faith.

(Narrative Discussion)

In the Tafsir of al-Qummi, regarding His saying, the Exalted: هُوَ الَّذِي أَخْرَجَ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا مِنْ أَهْلِ الْكِتابِ مِنْ دِيارِهِمْ "It is He who expelled the faithless from among the People of the Book from their homes..." he said: The reason for this was that there were three tribes of Jews in Medina: Banu al-Nadir, Qurayzah, and Qaynuqa'. There was a covenant and a term between them and the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, but they violated their covenant.

The reason for the Banu al-Nadir violating their covenant was that the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله came to them to seek an advance on the blood money (دية) for two men whom a man from his companions had killed by treachery—meaning he sought a loan. Among them was Ka'b ibn al-Ashraf. When he entered upon Ka'b, he said: "Welcome, O Abu al-Qasim!" and he stood up as if to prepare food for him, but he plotted within himself to kill the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله and pursue his companions. Then Gabriel descended and informed him of that.

The Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله returned to Medina and said to Muhammad ibn Maslamah al-Ansari: "Go to the Banu al-Nadir and inform them that Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, has informed me of the treachery you intended. Either you leave our land, or you permit war." They said: "We will leave your land."

Then Abdullah ibn Ubayy sent word to them: "Do not leave; stay and challenge Muhammad in war, for I will support you, I and my people and my allies. If you leave, I will leave with you, and if you fight, I will fight with you." So they stayed, repaired their fortresses among themselves, and prepared for battle. They sent word to the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله: "We will not leave; do what you will."

The Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله stood up and proclaimed the takbir, and his companions proclaimed the takbir. He said to the Commander of the Faithful: "Advance upon the Banu al-Nadir." The Commander of the Faithful took the banner and advanced. The Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله came and surrounded their fortress, and Abdullah ibn Ubayy betrayed them.

Whenever the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله appeared at the front of their houses, they would fortify what was adjacent to them and destroy what was behind it. A man among them who had a beautiful house would destroy it. The Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله had ordered the cutting of their palm trees, so they became distressed by that and said: "O Muhammad, does Allah command you to cause corruption? If this belongs to you, then take it; and if it belongs to us, then do not cut it."

When it was after that, they said: "O Muhammad, we will leave your land, so give us our wealth." He said: "No, but you shall leave, and you may have what the camels can carry." They did not accept that and remained for some days. Then they said: "We will leave, and we shall have what the camels can carry." He said: "No, but you shall leave and none of you shall carry anything. Whoever we find with anything of that, we shall kill him."

So they left on that condition. Some of them went to Fadak and Wadi al-Qura, and a group of them went to Syria.

Then Allah revealed concerning them: هُوَ الَّذِي أَخْرَجَ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا — until His saying — فَإِنَّ اللهَ شَدِيدُ الْعِقابِ. And Allah revealed to him regarding what they criticized of the cutting of the palm trees: ما قَطَعْتُمْ مِنْ لِينَةٍ أَوْ تَرَكْتُمُوها قائِمَةً عَلى أُصُولِها فَبِإِذْنِ اللهِ — until His saying — رَبَّنا إِنَّكَ رَؤُفٌ رَحِيمٌ.

And Allah revealed to him concerning Abdullah ibn Ubayy and his companions: أَلَمْ تَرَ إِلَى الَّذِينَ نافَقُوا — until His saying — ثُمَّ لا يُنْصَرُونَ.

In al-Majma', narrated from ibn Abbas: The Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله besieged them until he reached every possible extent with them. They gave him what he wanted from them, so he made peace with them on the condition that he would spare their lives, expel them from their land and homelands, and send them to Adhri'at in Syria. He assigned for every three of them a camel and a water-skin.

So they left for Adhri'at in Syria and Jericho, except for two households among them: the family of Abu al-Huqayq and the family of Huyayy ibn Akhtab, for they joined Khaybar, and a group of them joined al-Hirah.

In the same source, from Muhammad ibn Maslamah: That the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله sent him to the Banu al-Nadir and commanded him to give them a respite of three nights for the banishment.

In the same source, from Muhammad ibn Ishaq: The expulsion of the Banu al-Nadir was upon the Prophet's صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله return from Uhud, and the conquest of Qurayzah was upon his return from the [Battle of the] Confederates (الأحزاب). Al-Zuhri used to hold that the expulsion of the Banu al-Nadir was before Uhud, six months after the Battle of Badr.

In the same source, from ibn Abbas: The saying of the Exalted: ما أَفاءَ اللهُ عَلى رَسُولِهِ مِنْ أَهْلِ الْقُرى was revealed regarding the wealth of the faithless of the townships—and they were Qurayzah and Banu al-Nadir, both in Medina; Fadak, which is three miles from Medina; Khaybar; the villages of 'Uraynah; and Yanbu'. Allah made it for His Messenger to rule over as he wished, and He informed that it all belonged to him. Some people said: "Why does he not divide it?" So the verse was revealed.

In the same source, from ibn Abbas, he said: The Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله said on the day of Banu al-Nadir to the Helpers: "If you wish, you may divide for the Emigrants from your wealth and your homes and share with them in this booty; and if you wish, your homes and wealth shall be yours and nothing of the booty shall be divided for you." The Helpers said: "Rather, we will divide for them from our homes and wealth, and we will prefer them with the booty and not share with them in it." So there was revealed: وَيُؤْثِرُونَ عَلى أَنْفُسِهِمْ (and prefer [them] to themselves).

I say: Other stories have been narrated regarding their preference and the revelation of the verse concerning it. It appears that this is of the nature of applying the verse to the story. The previous meanings have been narrated in al-Durr al-Manthur through many different paths.

In al-Tawhid, narrated from Ali عليه‌السلام: He was asked about verses that were ambiguous to the questioner. He said regarding His saying, the Exalted: فَأَتاهُمُ اللهُ مِنْ حَيْثُ لَمْ يَحْتَسِبُوا — "It means He sent a punishment upon them."

In al-Tahdhib, with its chain of narration from al-Halabi from Abu Abdillah عليه‌السلام, he said regarding: ما أَفاءَ اللهُ عَلى رَسُولِهِ مِنْهُمْ فَما أَوْجَفْتُمْ عَلَيْهِ — "He said: The spoils (الفيء) are what consists of wealth in which there was no shedding of blood or killing, and the anfal (الأنفال) — spoils of war that belong to the Imam — are like that and are in its position."

In al-Majma', al-Minhal ibn 'Amr narrated from Ali ibn al-Husayn عليه‌السلام: I said: His saying: وَلِذِي الْقُرْبى وَالْيَتامى وَالْمَساكِينِ وَابْنِ السَّبِيلِ. He said: "They are our relatives, our needy, and our travellers."

I say: This meaning was narrated in al-Tahdhib from Sulaym ibn Qays from the Commander of the Faithful عليه‌السلام. In al-Majma', after quoting the previous narration, it says: "All the jurists said: they are the orphans of the people in general, and likewise the needy and the travellers. This has also been narrated from them عليه‌السلام."

In al-Kafi, with its chain of narration from Zurarah, that he heard Abu Ja'far and Abu Abdillah عليه‌السلام saying: "Indeed Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, delegated (فوض) to His Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله the affair of His creation to see how their obedience would be." Then he recited this verse: ما آتاكُمُ الرَّسُولُ فَخُذُوهُ وَما نَهاكُمْ عَنْهُ فَانْتَهُوا.

I say: The narrations from them عليه‌السلام regarding this meaning are numerous. The meaning of His delegating the affair of His creation to him—as appears from the narrations—is His endorsement of what the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله legislated for them and the obligation of obeying him in that, and his authority (ولايته) over the affairs of the people. As for delegation (التفويض) in the sense of Him, the Exalted, stripping that from Himself and entrusting it to him صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, that is impossible.

In the same source, with its chain of narration from Abu Abdillah عليه‌السلام in a tradition: "Faith is part of one another, and it is a domain (دار), and likewise Islam is a domain, and faithlessness is a domain."

In al-Mahasin, with its chain of narration from Abu Ubaydah from Abu Ja'far عليه‌السلام in a tradition, he said: "O Ziyad, woe to you! Is the religion anything but love? Do you not see the saying of Allah: إِنْ كُنْتُمْ تُحِبُّونَ اللهَ فَاتَّبِعُونِي يُحْبِبْكُمُ اللهُ وَيَغْفِرْ لَكُمْ ذُنُوبَكُمْ (If you love Allah, then follow me; Allah will love you and forgive you your sins)? Or do you not see the saying of Allah to Muhammad صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله: حَبَّبَ إِلَيْكُمُ الْإِيمانَ وَزَيَّنَهُ فِي قُلُوبِكُمْ (He has made faith dear to you and adorned it in your hearts)? And He said: يُحِبُّونَ مَنْ هاجَرَ إِلَيْهِمْ (who love those who migrate toward them). And he said: Religion is love, and love is religion."

In al-Majma', and in the tradition: "Greed (الشح) and faith do not gather in the heart of a Muslim man, nor do the dust in the way of Allah and the smoke of Hell gather in the belly of a Muslim man."

In al-Faqih, al-Fadl ibn Abi Qurrah al-Samandi narrated, saying: Abu Abdillah عليه‌السلام said to me: "Do you know who the greedy one (الشحيح) is?" I said: "He is the stingy one (البخيل)." He said: "Greed is more severe than stinginess. The stingy one is stingy with what is in his hand, but the greedy one is greedy for what is in the hands of the people and for what is in his own hand, until he sees nothing in the hands of the people except that he wishes it were his, by lawful or unlawful means, and he is not content with what Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, has provided him."

Ayahs

۞ أَلَمْ تَرَ إِلَى ٱلَّذِينَ نَافَقُوا۟ يَقُولُونَ لِإِخْوَٰنِهِمُ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ مِنْ أَهْلِ ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ لَئِنْ أُخْرِجْتُمْ لَنَخْرُجَنَّ مَعَكُمْ وَلَا نُطِيعُ فِيكُمْ أَحَدًا أَبَدًا وَإِن قُوتِلْتُمْ لَنَنصُرَنَّكُمْ وَٱللَّهُ يَشْهَدُ إِنَّهُمْ لَكَـٰذِبُونَ (١١)
Have you not regarded the hypocrites who say to their brethren, the faithless from among the People of the Book, ‘If you are expelled, we will surely go out with you, and we will never obey anyone against you, and if you are fought against we will surely help you,’ and Allah bears witness that they are indeed liars. (11)
لَئِنْ أُخْرِجُوا۟ لَا يَخْرُجُونَ مَعَهُمْ وَلَئِن قُوتِلُوا۟ لَا يَنصُرُونَهُمْ وَلَئِن نَّصَرُوهُمْ لَيُوَلُّنَّ ٱلْأَدْبَـٰرَ ثُمَّ لَا يُنصَرُونَ (١٢)
Surely, if they were expelled they will not go out with them, and if they were fought against they will not help them, and [even if] they were to help them they will turn their backs [to flee] and eventually they will not be helped. (12)
لَأَنتُمْ أَشَدُّ رَهْبَةً فِى صُدُورِهِم مِّنَ ٱللَّهِ ۚ ذَٰلِكَ بِأَنَّهُمْ قَوْمٌ لَّا يَفْقَهُونَ (١٣)
Indeed they have a greater awe of you in their hearts than of Allah. That is because they are a lot who do not understand. (13)
لَا يُقَـٰتِلُونَكُمْ جَمِيعًا إِلَّا فِى قُرًى مُّحَصَّنَةٍ أَوْ مِن وَرَآءِ جُدُرٍۭ ۚ بَأْسُهُم بَيْنَهُمْ شَدِيدٌ ۚ تَحْسَبُهُمْ جَمِيعًا وَقُلُوبُهُمْ شَتَّىٰ ۚ ذَٰلِكَ بِأَنَّهُمْ قَوْمٌ لَّا يَعْقِلُونَ (١٤)
They will not fight against you together except in fortified townships or from behind walls. Their fierceness is great only within themselves. You suppose them to be united, but their hearts are divided. That is because they are a lot who do not exercise their reason, (14)
كَمَثَلِ ٱلَّذِينَ مِن قَبْلِهِمْ قَرِيبًا ۖ ذَاقُوا۟ وَبَالَ أَمْرِهِمْ وَلَهُمْ عَذَابٌ أَلِيمٌ (١٥)
just like those who tasted the evil consequence of their conduct recently before them, and there is a painful punishment for them. (15)
كَمَثَلِ ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنِ إِذْ قَالَ لِلْإِنسَـٰنِ ٱكْفُرْ فَلَمَّا كَفَرَ قَالَ إِنِّى بَرِىٓءٌ مِّنكَ إِنِّىٓ أَخَافُ ٱللَّهَ رَبَّ ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ (١٦)
[The hypocrites are] like Satan when he tells man to disbelieve, but when he disbelieves, he says, ‘I am absolved of you. Indeed I fear Allah, the Lord of all the worlds.’ (16)
فَكَانَ عَـٰقِبَتَهُمَآ أَنَّهُمَا فِى ٱلنَّارِ خَـٰلِدَيْنِ فِيهَا ۚ وَذَٰلِكَ جَزَٰٓؤُا۟ ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ (١٧)
So the fate of both is that they will be in the Fire, to remain in it [forever]. Such is the requital of the wrongdoers. (17)

(Commentary)

This is an allusion to the state of the hypocrites (المنافقين) and their promise of victory to the Banu al-Nadir if they were fought, and of departing with them if they were expelled, as well as Allah’s denunciation of them as liars regarding what they promised.

His saying, the Exalted: أَلَمْ تَرَ إِلَى الَّذِينَ نافَقُوا يَقُولُونَ لِإِخْوانِهِمُ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا مِنْ أَهْلِ الْكِتابِ "Have you not regarded the hypocrites who say to their brethren who are faithless among the People of the Book" (59:11), etc. The term "brethren" (الإخوان), like al-ikhwah (الأخوة), is a plural of "brother" (أخ). Brotherhood (الأخوة) is the sharing of lineage from a single father, but it is used more broadly to refer to those who share a common belief, friendship, or the like. It is said that al-ikhwah (الأخوة) is most frequently used for those sharing paternal lineage, while al-ikhwan (الإخوان) is used for those sharing a belief and similar ties.

The interrogative in the verse is for expressing wonder. Those intended by "the hypocrites" (الذين نافقوا) are ‘Abdullah ibn Ubayy and his companions. Those intended by "their brethren who are faithless among the People of the Book" (إخوانهم الذين كفروا من أهل الكتاب) are the Banu al-Nadir, as supported by the context. Indeed, the import of the verses is that they were a group from the People of the Book whose affair fluctuated between expulsion and fighting, following another group who were in a similar situation; this refers to none other than the Banu al-Nadir following the Banu Qaynuqa‘.

His saying: لَئِنْ أُخْرِجْتُمْ لَنَخْرُجَنَّ مَعَكُمْ وَلا نُطِيعُ فِيكُمْ أَحَداً أَبَداً وَإِنْ قُوتِلْتُمْ لَنَنْصُرَنَّكُمْ "‘If you are expelled, we will surely leave with you, and we will never obey anyone against you; and if you are fought, we will surely help you.’" (59:11) is the content of the hypocrites' speech. The lam in "If you are expelled" (لَئِنْ أُخْرِجْتُمْ) is for an oath; meaning: We swear that if the Muslims expel you from your homes, we will surely leave our homes with you, remaining by your side, and we will never obey anyone regarding you—that is, regarding your affair—who advises us to part from you, and if the Muslims fight you, we will surely help you against them.

His saying: وَاللهُ يَشْهَدُ إِنَّهُمْ لَكاذِبُونَ "But Allah bears witness that they are indeed liars." (59:11) is a refutation of the hypocrites' promise and an explicit declaration that they will not fulfill their word.

His saying, the Exalted: لَئِنْ أُخْرِجُوا لا يَخْرُجُونَ مَعَهُمْ وَلَئِنْ قُوتِلُوا لا يَنْصُرُونَهُمْ "If they are expelled, they will not leave with them, and if they are fought, they will not help them." (59:12) is a detailed refutation of their promise following the general refutation in His saying: "But Allah bears witness that they are indeed liars." The lam of the oath is repeated here, and the meaning is: I swear that if the Banu al-Nadir are expelled, the hypocrites will not leave with them; and I swear that if they are fought, they will not help them.

His saying, the Exalted: وَلَئِنْ نَصَرُوهُمْ لَيُوَلُّنَّ الْأَدْبارَ ثُمَّ لا يُنْصَرُونَ "And if they help them, they will surely turn their backs [to flee], then they will not be helped." (59:12) is an indication that their help, on the supposition that it were to occur—though it never shall—would neither last nor benefit them. Rather, they would turn their backs in flight, and then they would not be helped; instead, they would perish without anyone to assist them.

His saying, the Exalted: لَأَنْتُمْ أَشَدُّ رَهْبَةً فِي صُدُورِهِمْ مِنَ اللهِ "You are a greater cause of terror in their breasts than Allah." (59:13), etc. The plural pronouns refer to the hypocrites. Terror (الرهبة) is fear (الخشية). The verse serves as a justification for His saying: "And if they help them, they will surely turn their backs"; meaning: that is because they fear you more than they fear Allah, so they will not resist you if you fight them, nor will they stand firm against you.

He explained the reason for this by His saying: ذلِكَ بِأَنَّهُمْ قَوْمٌ لا يَفْقَهُونَ "That is because they are a people who do not understand." (59:13). The word "that" (ذلك) refers to the fact that their terror of the believers is greater than their terror of Allah. That is, their terror of you is such because they are a people who do not understand with true understanding. Had they understood the reality of the matter, they would have known that all affairs belong to Allah, the Exalted, and no one else has any share in the matter—whether they be Muslims or others. No one besides Him, the Exalted, has the power to bring about good or evil, benefit or harm, except by a power and strength from Him, the Exalted. Therefore, none should be feared except Him, the Mighty and Majestic.

His saying, the Exalted: لا يُقاتِلُونَكُمْ جَمِيعاً إِلَّا فِي قُرىً مُحَصَّنَةٍ أَوْ مِنْ وَراءِ جُدُرٍ "They will not fight you together except in fortified townships, or from behind walls." (59:14) is an explanation of the effect of their collective terror and cowardice. The meaning is: The Banu al-Nadir and the hypocrites will not fight you collectively by coming out into the open; rather, they will only do so within fortified, reinforced townships or from behind walls without emerging.

His saying: بَأْسُهُمْ بَيْنَهُمْ شَدِيدٌ "Their might is great among themselves." (59:14) means they possess great prowess and ferocity among one another, yet when they come out to war against you and see you, they turn cowardly due to the dread Allah has cast into their hearts.

His saying: تَحْسَبُهُمْ جَمِيعاً وَقُلُوبُهُمْ شَتَّى "You suppose them to be a whole, but their hearts are scattered." (59:14) means you might think they are united in affection and solidarity, whereas in reality, their hearts are divided and lack unity. This is the greatest factor leading to disgrace and abandonment. ذلِكَ بِأَنَّهُمْ قَوْمٌ لا يَعْقِلُونَ "That is because they are a people who do not exercise their reason." (59:14); for had they used their reason, they would have united and harmonized their word.

His saying, the Exalted: كَمَثَلِ الَّذِينَ مِنْ قَبْلِهِمْ قَرِيباً ذاقُوا وَبالَ أَمْرِهِمْ وَلَهُمْ عَذابٌ أَلِيمٌ "Like those who were shortly before them: they tasted the evil consequence of their conduct, and there is a painful punishment for them." (59:15). The "evil consequence" (الوبال) is the bad outcome. The word "shortly" (قريباً) stands in place of an adverb of time, meaning "in a recent time."

His saying: "Like those..." (كَمَثَلِ) is the predicate of a suppressed subject, the estimation being: "Their likeness is like the likeness of..." (مثلهم كمثل). The meaning is: The likeness of them—that is, the likeness of the Banu al-Nadir from the Jews in their breaking of the covenant and the hypocrites' false promise of victory to them, followed by their exile—is like the likeness of those who were before them in a recent time. These were the Banu Qaynuqa‘, another clan of the Jews of Madinah who broke the covenant after the Battle of Badr. The Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله expelled them to Adhru‘at. The hypocrites had promised them that they would speak to the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله on their behalf and prevent him from expelling them, but they betrayed them. Thus, the Banu Qaynuqa‘ tasted the evil consequence of their conduct, and for them in the Hereafter is a painful punishment. It is also said that those intended by "those before them" are the faithless of Makkah on the day of Badr, but the former view is more consistent with the context.

In any case, the parable is a likeness for the Banu al-Nadir, not for the hypocrites, as indicated by the context.

His saying, the Exalted: كَمَثَلِ الشَّيْطانِ إِذْ قالَ لِلْإِنْسانِ اكْفُرْ فَلَمَّا كَفَرَ قالَ إِنِّي بَرِيءٌ مِنْكَ "Like the Devil when he says to man, ‘Disbelieve.’ Then when he disbelieves, he says, ‘I am indeed quit of you.’" (59:16), etc. The apparent context suggests this is a parable for the hypocrites in their deceiving the Banu al-Nadir with the promise of victory and then abandoning them in their time of need.

The apparent context also suggests that "the Devil" (الشيطان) and "man" (الإنسان) refer to the genus. It alludes to the Devil's deception of man by calling him to disbelief (الكفر) through the beautification of the enjoyments of life and the enticement to turn away from the truth with his false promises and mirage-like hopes. When the precursors of the Hereafter appear to him and he witnesses that the worldly hopes he was deceived by were nothing but a mirage that deluded him and a fantasy that toyed with him, the Devil disavows him and fails to fulfill what he promised, saying: "I am indeed quit of you; indeed I fear Allah, the Lord of all the worlds."

In short, the likeness of the hypocrites in their calling the Banu al-Nadir to oppose the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله and promising them victory, then betraying them and breaking the promise, is like the likeness of this Devil in calling man to disbelief with his false promises, then disavowing him after his disbelief when he is in need.

It is said that the parable refers to the story of Barsisa the worshiper, for whom the Devil beautified immorality, so he committed adultery with a woman and then disbelieved. This story will be mentioned in the following traditional discussion, Allah willing.

It is also said that the previous parable mentioned in His saying: "Like those who were shortly before them" refers to the faithless of Makkah on the day of Badr—as mentioned before—and that "man" in this parable refers to Abu Jahl. The Devil's saying to him "Disbelieve" refers to what Allah narrated in the story: وَإِذْ زَيَّنَ لَهُمُ الشَّيْطانُ أَعْمالَهُمْ وَقالَ لا غالِبَ لَكُمُ الْيَوْمَ مِنَ النَّاسِ وَإِنِّي جارٌ لَكُمْ فَلَمَّا تَراءَتِ الْفِئَتانِ نَكَصَ عَلى عَقِبَيْهِ وَقالَ إِنِّي بَرِيءٌ مِنْكُمْ إِنِّي أَرى ما لا تَرَوْنَ إِنِّي أَخافُ اللهَ وَاللهُ شَدِيدُ الْعِقابِ "And when Satan made their deeds seem decorous to them, and said, ‘No one shall overcome you today from among the people, and I will be your protector.’ But when the two hosts sighted each other, he took to his heels, and said, ‘I am indeed quit of you. I see what you do not see. I indeed fear Allah, and Allah is severe in retribution.’" (8:48).

According to this view, the Devil's saying: "I indeed fear Allah, the Lord of all the worlds" is a serious statement, because he feared the punishment of the angels who descended to help the believers at Badr. As for the first two views, it is a form of mockery and humiliation.

His saying, the Exalted: فَكانَ عاقِبَتَهُما أَنَّهُما فِي النَّارِ خالِدَيْنِ فِيها وَذلِكَ جَزاءُ الظَّالِمِينَ "So the outcome for both of them is that they will be in the Fire, to remain in it [forever], and that is the requital of the wrongdoers." (59:17). It appears that the dual pronouns refer to the Devil and the man mentioned in the parable. Thus, the verse explains the outcome of the Devil in his deceiving and misleading man, and of man in his being deceived by him and going astray. It also alludes to the fact that this is the outcome of the hypocrites in their promise to the Banu al-Nadir and their betrayal of them, and the outcome of the Banu al-Nadir in being deceived by their false promise and their persistence in opposition and hostility. The meaning of the verse is clear.

(Narrative Discussion)

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Ibn Ishaq, Ibn al-Mundhir, and Abu Nu‘aym in al-Dala’il narrated from Ibn ‘Abbas: A group from the Banu ‘Awf ibn al-Harith—among them ‘Abdullah ibn Ubayy ibn Salul, Wadi‘ah ibn Malik, Suwayd, and Da‘is—sent word to the Banu al-Nadir: "Stand firm and defend yourselves, for we shall not surrender you. If you are fought, we shall fight with you, and if you are expelled, we shall leave with you." So they waited for that help from them, but they did not do it, and Allah cast terror into their hearts.

They then asked the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله to expel them and spare their lives, on the condition that they could take whatever their camels could carry of their wealth except for the armor (الحلقة). He agreed, and a man among them would demolish his house and place it on the back of his camel to depart with it. They left for Khaybar, and some of them traveled to Syria.

I say: This narration contradicts several other narrations stating that the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله was the one who initially offered them to leave with what their camels could carry of wealth, but they did not accept. Then, after some days, they agreed to it, but the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله did not accept—except that they leave with only themselves and their families without carrying anything. So they left in that manner, and the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله assigned one camel and a water-skin for every three of them.

In the same source, Ibn Mardawayh narrated from Ibn ‘Abbas regarding: أَلَمْ تَرَ إِلَى الَّذِينَ نافَقُوا "Have you not regarded the hypocrites": He said: "‘Abdullah ibn Ubayy ibn Salul, Rifa‘ah ibn Tabut, ‘Abdullah ibn Nabtal, and Aws ibn Qayzi." And regarding "their brethren" (لِإِخْوانِهِمْ): "The Banu al-Nadir."

I say: This is intended to name some of them, so it does not contradict what was in the previous narration.

In the same source, Ibn Abi al-Dunya in Maka’id al-Shaytan, Ibn Mardawayh, and al-Bayhaqi in Shu‘ab al-Iman narrated from ‘Ubayd ibn Rifa‘ah al-Darimi, reaching the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, who said: "There was a monk among the Children of Israel. The Devil took a young girl and strangled her, then cast into the hearts of her family that her cure was with the monk. They brought her to the monk, but he refused to accept her. They persisted until he accepted her, and she stayed with him.

Then the Devil came to him, whispered to him, and beautified [the sin] for him. He did not leave him until he fell upon her. When she became pregnant, the Devil whispered to him and said: 'Now you will be disgraced; her family will come to you. Kill her, and if they come to you, say: She died.' So he killed her and buried her. Then the Devil went to her family, whispered to them, and cast into their hearts that he had impregnated her and then killed her. Her family came to him and asked him, and he said: 'She died.' So they seized him.

The Devil then came to him and said: 'I am the one who cast [this] into the hearts of her family, and I am the one who caused you to fall into this. Obey me and you shall be saved; prostrate to me twice.' So he prostrated to him twice. He is the one about whom Allah said: كَمَثَلِ الشَّيْطانِ إِذْ قالَ لِلْإِنْسانِ اكْفُرْ 'Like the Devil when he says to man, "Disbelieve..."' the verse."

I say: The story is famous and has been narrated in both brief and detailed forms in many narrations.

Ayahs

يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ ٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ وَلْتَنظُرْ نَفْسٌ مَّا قَدَّمَتْ لِغَدٍ ۖ وَٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ خَبِيرٌۢ بِمَا تَعْمَلُونَ (١٨)
O you who have faith! Be wary of Allah, and let every soul consider what it sends ahead for Tomorrow, and be wary of Allah. Allah is indeed well aware of what you do (18)
وَلَا تَكُونُوا۟ كَٱلَّذِينَ نَسُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ فَأَنسَىٰهُمْ أَنفُسَهُمْ ۚ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ هُمُ ٱلْفَـٰسِقُونَ (١٩)
Do not be like those who forget Allah, so He makes them forget their own souls. It is they who are the transgressors. (19)
لَا يَسْتَوِىٓ أَصْحَـٰبُ ٱلنَّارِ وَأَصْحَـٰبُ ٱلْجَنَّةِ ۚ أَصْحَـٰبُ ٱلْجَنَّةِ هُمُ ٱلْفَآئِزُونَ (٢٠)
Not equal are the inmates of the Fire and the inhabitants of paradise. It is the inhabitants of paradise who are the successful ones. (20)
لَوْ أَنزَلْنَا هَـٰذَا ٱلْقُرْءَانَ عَلَىٰ جَبَلٍ لَّرَأَيْتَهُۥ خَـٰشِعًا مُّتَصَدِّعًا مِّنْ خَشْيَةِ ٱللَّهِ ۚ وَتِلْكَ ٱلْأَمْثَـٰلُ نَضْرِبُهَا لِلنَّاسِ لَعَلَّهُمْ يَتَفَكَّرُونَ (٢١)
Had We sent down this Quran upon a mountain, you would have seen it humbled [and] go to pieces with the fear of Allah. We draw such comparisons for mankind, so that they may reflect. (21)
هُوَ ٱللَّهُ ٱلَّذِى لَآ إِلَـٰهَ إِلَّا هُوَ ۖ عَـٰلِمُ ٱلْغَيْبِ وَٱلشَّهَـٰدَةِ ۖ هُوَ ٱلرَّحْمَـٰنُ ٱلرَّحِيمُ (٢٢)
He is Allah—there is no god except Him—Knower of the sensible and the Unseen, He is the All-beneficent, the All-merciful. (22)
هُوَ ٱللَّهُ ٱلَّذِى لَآ إِلَـٰهَ إِلَّا هُوَ ٱلْمَلِكُ ٱلْقُدُّوسُ ٱلسَّلَـٰمُ ٱلْمُؤْمِنُ ٱلْمُهَيْمِنُ ٱلْعَزِيزُ ٱلْجَبَّارُ ٱلْمُتَكَبِّرُ ۚ سُبْحَـٰنَ ٱللَّهِ عَمَّا يُشْرِكُونَ (٢٣)
He is Allah—there is no god except Him—the Sovereign, the All-holy, the All-benign, the Securer, the All-conserver, the All-mighty, the All-compeller, and the All-magnanimous. Clear is Allah of any partners that they may ascribe [to Him]! (23)
هُوَ ٱللَّهُ ٱلْخَـٰلِقُ ٱلْبَارِئُ ٱلْمُصَوِّرُ ۖ لَهُ ٱلْأَسْمَآءُ ٱلْحُسْنَىٰ ۚ يُسَبِّحُ لَهُۥ مَا فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ ۖ وَهُوَ ٱلْعَزِيزُ ٱلْحَكِيمُ (٢٤)
He is Allah, the Creator, the Maker, and the Former. To Him belong the Best Names. Whatever there is in the heavens and the earth glorifies Him and He is the All-mighty, the All-wise. (24)

(Commentary)

The content of these noble verses serves as a conclusion drawn from the preceding verses of the Surah. Reference was made therein to the opposition (مشاقة) of the Banu al-Nadir among the Jews and their breaking of the covenant—which cast them into loss in this world and the Hereafter—as well as the incitement by the hypocrites for them to oppose Allah and His Messenger, which led to their destruction. The true cause behind this was that they did not remain mindful of Allah in their actions and forgot Him; consequently, He caused them to forget their own souls. Thus, they did not choose what contained the good of their souls or their welfare in the immediate or the distant future, and so they wandered aimlessly and perished.

Therefore, it is incumbent upon whoever believes in Allah, His Messenger, and the Last Day to remember his Lord and not forget Him, and to look into what deeds he puts forward for the Day of return to his Lord. Indeed, what he has performed is preserved against him; Allah will call him to account for it on that Day and recompense him with a necessary recompense that shall never leave him.

This is what is intended by His saying: يَا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا اتَّقُوا اللَّهَ وَلْتَنظُرْ نَفْسٌ مَّا قَدَّمَتْ لِغَدٍ "O you who have faith! Be wary of Allah, and let every soul consider what it sends ahead for tomorrow" (59:18). These verses exhort the believers to remember Allah, the Glorified, and not forget Him, and to examine their deeds—upon the righteousness or corruption of which the millstone of their afterlife turns. They should maintain a continuous vigilance (مراقبة) over their actions to ensure they are righteous and sincere for His Noble Face, then call their souls to account (محاسبة), thanking Allah for the good they have done, and rebuking and restraining the soul for the evils it has perpetrated, while seeking forgiveness.

Remembering Allah (ذكر الله) in a manner befitting the courtyard of His Greatness and Majesty—through His Beautiful Names (الأسماء الحسنى) and Sublime Attributes (الصفات العليا) which the Noble Quran has elucidated in its teaching—is the only path that leads its wayfarer to the perfection of servitude (كمال العبودية), and there is no perfection for man above it.

This is because man is a pure servant (عبد محض) and an absolute possession (مملوك طلق) of Allah, the Glorified. He is owned from every conceivable aspect and possesses no independence (استقلال) from any side, just as He, the Exalted, is his Owner from every conceivable aspect and possesses independence from every side. The perfection of a thing lies in its purity in its essence and its effects. Thus, the perfection of man lies in seeing himself as owned by Allah without any independence, and in being characterized by the attributes of servitude—such as submission (خضوع), humility (خشوع), lowliness (ذلة), submissiveness (استكانة), and poverty (فقر)—in relation to the courtyard of Greatness, Might, and Self-Sufficiency (الغنى). Furthermore, his actions and deeds should proceed according to what Allah wills, not what his own soul desires, without any heedlessness in any of these stages: essence (الذات), attributes (الصفات), and actions (الأفعال).

His viewing of his own essence, attributes, and actions with a gaze of pure dependency and absolute ownership cannot be realized except through an inward turning (التوجه الباطني) toward his Lord, Who is a Witness (شهيد) over all things and encompasses (محيط) all things, and Who is the Maintainer (القائم) of every soul as to what it has earned, without ever being heedless of Him or forgetting Him.

At that moment, his heart finds tranquility, as He, the Exalted, said: أَلَا بِذِكْرِ اللَّهِ تَطْمَئِنُّ الْقُلُوبُ "Look! The hearts find rest in Allah’s remembrance!" (13:28). He comes to know Allah, the Glorified, through His attributes of perfection contained within His Beautiful Names, and in response to that, the attributes of his own servitude and aspects of deficiency manifest from him—such as submission, humility, lowliness, poverty, and need.

This is followed by his righteous deeds through the permanence of presence (دوام الحضور) and the continuity of remembrance (استمرار الذكر). The Exalted said: وَاذْكُر رَّبَّكَ فِي نَفْسِكَ تَضَرُّعًا وَخِيفَةً وَدُونَ الْجَهْرِ مِنَ الْقَوْلِ بِالْغُدُوِّ وَالْآصَالِ وَلَا تَكُن مِّنَ الْغَافِلِينَ إِنَّ الَّذِينَ عِندَ رَبِّكَ لَا يَسْتَكْبِرُونَ عَنْ عِبَادَتِهِ وَيُسَبِّحُونَهُ وَلَهُ يَسْجُدُونَ "And remember your Lord within your self submissively and fearfully, and without raising your voice, at morning and evening, and do not be among the heedless. Indeed those who are [stationed] near your Lord do not disdain His worship, and they glorify Him and prostrate to Him" (7:205-206). And He said: فَإِنِ اسْتَكْبَرُوا فَالَّذِينَ عِندَ رَبِّكَ يُسَبِّحُونَ لَهُ بِاللَّيْلِ وَالنَّهَارِ وَهُمْ لَا يَسْأَمُونَ "But if they are disdainful, those who are near your Lord glorify Him night and day, and they do not tire" (41:38).

To what we have mentioned regarding knowing Him, the Exalted, through His attributes of perfection and knowing the soul through its corresponding attributes of deficiency and need, His saying points by the requirement of the context: لَوْ أَنزَلْنَا هَٰذَا الْقُرْآنَ "Had We sent down this Quran..." (59:21) until the end of the verses.

His saying, the Exalted: يَا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا اتَّقُوا اللَّهَ وَلْتَنظُرْ نَفْسٌ مَّا قَدَّمَتْ لِغَدٍ "O you who have faith! Be wary of Allah, and let every soul consider what it sends ahead for tomorrow" (59:18) until the end of the verse. This is a command to the believers to have godwariness (تقوى) — the protective shield of the soul through obedience to Allah — and another command, which is to look into the deeds they have sent forward for the Day of Reckoning: are they righteous, such that they may hope for Allah's reward through them, or are they corrupt, such that they should fear Allah's punishment for them and rectify them through repentance (التوبة) and turning back (الإنابة)? This is the accounting of the soul (محاسبة النفس).

As for godwariness (التقوى), it has been explained in the traditions as "piety regarding the prohibitions of Allah" (الورع عن محارم الله). Since it relates to both obligations and prohibitions together, it constitutes the avoidance of neglecting obligations and the avoidance of committing prohibitions.

As for "considering what the soul has sent ahead for tomorrow," it is another matter beyond godwariness. Its relation to godwariness is like the relation of a secondary corrective gaze by a worker toward his work or a craftsman toward what he has fashioned, in order to perfect it and rectify the deficiencies he was heedless of or the mistakes he made during the process of work and production.

Thus, it is incumbent upon all believers to be wary of Allah regarding the duties directed toward them, obeying Him and not disobeying Him, and then to look into the deeds they have sent forward—by which they shall live tomorrow after being called to account for them—whether they are righteous, so their reward is hoped for, or corrupt, so their punishment is feared, and thus they repent to Allah and seek His forgiveness.

This is a general duty that encompasses every believer because of the universal need to rectify one's deeds, and the consideration of some does not suffice for the consideration of others. However, those among the people of faith who actually perform this are extremely few, to the point of being almost non-existent. To this, the phrasing of the verse alludes: وَلْتَنظُرْ نَفْسٌ "and let a soul consider."

So His saying: وَلْتَنظُرْ نَفْسٌ مَّا قَدَّمَتْ لِغَدٍ "and let every soul consider what it sends ahead for tomorrow" is a general address to all believers. However, since those occupied with this consideration among the people of faith—indeed, even among the people of godwariness—are in the utmost minority, nearly non-existent due to their preoccupation with the fleeting interests of the world and the absorption of their time in managing livelihoods and rectifying the affairs of life, the address was delivered in the third person (الغيبة) and attached to an indefinite soul (نفس ما), saying: "and let a soul consider." In this type of address, despite the duty being general by nature, there is a rebuke and a reprimand for the believers, along with an allusion to the scarcity of those among them who are fit to comply with it.

His saying: مَا قَدَّمَتْ لِغَدٍ "what it sends ahead for tomorrow" is an inquiry into the nature of the deed sent ahead for tomorrow and an elucidation of the "consideration." It is also possible that مَا is a relative pronoun, and it, along with its relative clause, is the object of the "consideration."

What is meant by "tomorrow" (غد) is the Day of Resurrection, which is the day of the reckoning of deeds. It was expressed as "tomorrow" to indicate its proximity to them, like the proximity of tomorrow to its yesterday. The Exalted said: إِنَّهُمْ يَرَوْنَهُ بَعِيدًا وَنَرَاهُ قَرِيبًا "Indeed they see it to be far off, and We see it to be near" (70:6-7).

The meaning is: O you who have faith, be wary of Allah by obeying Him in all that He commands you and forbids you, and let a soul among you look into what it has performed of deeds, and let it see what it has sent forward of its actions for the Day of Reckoning—is it a righteous deed or a corrupt one? And is its righteous deed truly righteous and accepted, or is it rejected?

His saying: وَاتَّقُوا اللَّهَ إِنَّ اللَّهَ خَبِيرٌ بِمَا تَعْمَلُونَ "and be wary of Allah. Indeed Allah is well aware of what you do" (59:18) is a second command for godwariness, and إِنَّ اللَّهَ خَبِيرٌ "Indeed Allah is well aware..." is its justification. Justifying this godwariness by His being aware of the deeds indicates that the godwariness commanded a second time is godwariness in the station of accounting and considering deeds in terms of their rectification, their sincerity for Allah, the Glorified, and their preservation from what corrupts them. As for His saying at the beginning of the verse: اتَّقُوا اللَّهَ "Be wary of Allah," what is meant is godwariness in the very performance of deeds by restricting them to acts of obedience and avoiding acts of disobedience.

From here it becomes clear that the meaning of godwariness in the two instances is different: the first is godwariness in the original performance of deeds, and the second is godwariness regarding the performed deeds in terms of their rectification and sincerity.

It also becomes apparent that the statement of some—that the first is for repentance from past sins and the second is for avoiding future disobedience—is not sound. Similar is what has been said: that the first is for performing obligations and the second is for abandoning prohibitions; or that the second command is merely for the emphasis of the first.

His saying, the Exalted: وَلَا تَكُونُوا كَالَّذِينَ نَسُوا اللَّهَ فَأَنسَاهُمْ أَنفُسَهُمْ "And do not be like those who forgot Allah, so He made them forget their own souls" (59:19) etc. Forgetfulness (النسيان) is the disappearance of the form of the known from the soul after it had been present therein, along with the disappearance of its origin. It is used broadly to refer to any turning away (الإعراض) from a thing by not according it any practical effect. The Exalted said: وَقِيلَ الْيَوْمَ نَنسَاكُمْ كَمَا نَسِيتُمْ لِقَاءَ يَوْمِكُمْ هَٰذَا وَمَأْوَاكُمُ النَّارُ وَمَا لَكُم مِّن نَّاصِرِينَ "It will be said, ‘Today We will forget you just as you forgot the encounter of this day of yours. Your abode will be the Fire, and you will not have any helpers’" (45:34).

The verse, according to its core meaning, is like an emphasis on the content of the previous verse, as if it were said: Send forward for the Day of Reckoning and Recompense a righteous deed by which your souls shall live, and do not forget it. Furthermore, since the cause of forgetting the soul is forgetting Allah—for by forgetting Him, the Exalted, His Beautiful Names and Sublime Attributes are forgotten, to which the essential attributes of man (such as lowliness, poverty, and need) are linked—man then imagines himself to be independent in existence. He fancies that he possesses for himself life, power, knowledge, and the rest of the perfections that appear to him, and he views the other apparent cosmic causes as his peers in independence, affecting him and being affected by him.

At that point, he relies upon himself, whereas he ought to have relied upon his Lord; he hopes in and fears the apparent causes, whereas he ought to have hoped in and feared his Lord; he finds tranquility in other than his Lord, whereas he ought to have found tranquility in his Lord.

In short, he forgets his Lord and the return to Him, and he turns away from Him by turning toward others. From this follows that he forgets his soul; for what he fancies of his soul—that it is an independently existing being, owning the perfections of existence that appear in it, and that the management of its affairs belongs to it, drawing support from the cosmic causes around it—is not the human being. Rather, the human being is a creature whose existence is dependent (متعلق الوجود), who is total ignorance, total weakness, total lowliness, total poverty, and so on. Whatever perfection he possesses—such as existence, knowledge, power, honor, and self-sufficiency—belongs to his Lord and terminates in his Lord, and the other cosmic causes are like him in that regard.

In conclusion, since the cause of forgetting the soul is forgetting Allah, the prohibition of forgetting the soul in the verse was shifted to a prohibition of forgetting Him, the Exalted, because the cessation of the effect through the cessation of its cause is more eloquent and emphatic. He did not settle for a mere general prohibition of forgetting Him—by saying, "and do not forget Allah, lest He make you forget yourselves"—but rather proceeded by giving the ruling through an example to be more effective and closer to acceptance. Thus, He forbade them from being like those who forgot Allah, referring to those mentioned previously among the Jews of Banu al-Nadir and Banu Qaynuqa' and those whose state is like theirs in opposing Allah and His Messenger.

So He said: وَلَا تَكُونُوا كَالَّذِينَ نَسُوا اللَّهَ "And do not be like those who forgot Allah," then He branched from it His saying: فأنساهُمْ أنفُسَهُمْ "so He made them forget their own souls," as the branching of an effect from its cause. Then He followed it with His saying: أُولَٰئِكَ هُمُ الْفَاسِقُونَ "it is they who are the transgressors," indicating that they are truly transgressors (فاسقون), having exited the garb of servitude.

The verse, although it forbids forgetting Him—which results in forgetting the soul—nevertheless, by its occurrence in the context of the previous verse, commands the remembrance of Allah and vigilance (مراقبة) over Him.

It has become clear from all that preceded in the two verses that the first verse commands the accounting of the soul (محاسبة النفس) and the second commands remembrance (الذكر) and vigilance (المراقبة).

His saying, the Exalted: لَا يَسْتَوِي أَصْحَابُ النَّارِ وَأَصْحَابُ الْجَنَّةِ أَصْحَابُ الْجَنَّةِ هُمُ الْفَائِزُونَ "The inhabitants of the Fire and the inhabitants of paradise are not equal. It is the inhabitants of paradise who are the winners" (59:20). Al-Raghib said: Success (الفوز) is the attainment of good along with the achievement of safety. The context bears witness that what is meant by "the inhabitants of the Fire" are those who forget Allah, and by "the inhabitants of Paradise" are those who remember Allah and are vigilant.

The verse is a complete proof for the necessity of joining those who remember Allah and are vigilant over Him, rather than those who forget. Its formulation is that there are two groups and no third: those who remember Allah and those who forget Him. Man must necessarily join one of the two. They are not equal such that the two joinings would be equal and man would not care which he joined. Rather, there is a preferred and a non-preferred, and it is mandatory to choose the preferred over the non-preferred. Preference belongs to the group of those who remember, for they are the successful ones and none else. Thus, the preference is for their side, so it is mandatory for every soul to choose to join the group of those who remember.

His saying, the Exalted: لَوْ أَنزَلْنَا هَٰذَا الْقُرْآنَ عَلَىٰ جَبَلٍ لَّرَأَيْتَهُ خَاشِعًا مُّتَصَدِّعًا مِّنْ خَشْيَةِ اللَّهِ "Had We sent down this Quran upon a mountain, you would have seen it humbled [and] rent asunder out of the fear of Allah" (59:21) etc. In al-Majma': Splitting (التصدع) is the separation after cohesion, similar to "cleaving asunder" (التفطر).

The speech is presented as a parable (مثل) based on imagery (تخييل), and the proof for this is His saying at the end of the verse: وَتِلْكَ الْأَمْثَالُ نَضْرِبُهَا لِلنَّاسِ "We draw such parables for mankind..." (59:21).

The intent is to magnify the matter of the Quran by virtue of the realities of knowledge, the foundations of laws, the lessons, the exhortations, the promises, and the threats it contains—it being the speech of Allah, the Great. The meaning is: If the mountain were something upon which it were permissible to send down the Quran, and We sent it down upon it, you would have seen it—despite its hardness, its harshness, its massive size, and its strength of resistance against calamities—affected and rent asunder out of the fear of Allah. If this is the state of the mountain as it is, then man is more worthy of being humbled before Allah when he recites it or it is recited to him. How strange is the state of the people of opposition and stubbornness; their hearts do not soften for it, nor do they humble themselves or fear.

The shift (التفات) from the first-person plural ("We") to the third person in His saying: مِنْ خَشْيَةِ اللَّهِ "out of the fear of Allah" is to indicate the cause of the ruling; for the mountain only humbles itself and rends asunder at the descent of the Quran because it is the speech of Allah, exalted is His Name.

His saying: وَتِلْكَ الْأَمْثَالُ نَضْرِبُهَا لِلنَّاسِ لَعَلَّهُمْ يَتَفَكَّرُونَ "We draw such parables for mankind so that they may reflect" (59:21) is the placement of a universal ruling in the place of a particular one to indicate that the ruling is not unique to its instance but is applicable and pervasive in many other instances.

So His saying: لَوْ أَنزَلْنَا هَٰذَا الْقُرْآنَ عَلَىٰ جَبَلٍ "Had We sent down this Quran upon a mountain..." is a parable Allah has drawn for mankind regarding the matter of the Quran to bring closer its greatness and the majesty of its status—inasmuch as it is the speech of Allah and because of the knowledge it contains—in the hope that people will reflect upon it. Thus, they would receive the Quran with the reception it deserves, realize the manifest truth within it, and be guided to what it leads to of the path of servitude (طريق العبودية), beyond which there is no path to their perfection and happiness. Included in this is what was mentioned in the previous verses regarding vigilance (المراقبة) and accounting (المحاسبة).

His saying, the Exalted: هُوَ اللَّهُ الَّذِي لَا إِلَٰهَ إِلَّا هُوَ عَالِمُ الْغَيْبِ وَالشَّهَادَةِ هُوَ الرَّحْمَٰنُ الرَّحِيمُ "He is Allah—there is no god except Him—Knower of the Unseen and the Sensible, He is the All-beneficent, the All-merciful" (59:22). This verse and the two verses after it, although presented to enumerate a group of His Beautiful Names and to point to His being named by every most beautiful name and His being glorified by the testimony of what is in the heavens and the earth, nevertheless, when joined with the preceding command for remembrance, they imply that those who remember should remember Him by His Beautiful Names and thus know their own souls through the corresponding names of deficiency. Understand this.

And by joining them with the previous verse and what it contains of His saying: مِنْ خَشْيَةِ اللَّهِ "out of the fear of Allah," they serve to justify the mountain's humbling and rending asunder out of the fear of Allah. It is as if it were said: And how could it not be so, when He is Allah, besides Whom there is no god, the Knower of the Unseen and the Sensible... until the end of the verses.

His saying: هُوَ اللَّهُ الَّذِي لَا إِلَٰهَ إِلَّا هُوَ "He is Allah—there is no god except Him" provides, through the relative pronoun and its clause, the meaning of one of His names, which is His Oneness (وحدانيته) in His divinity and His being the only object of worship. Some of what relates to the declaration of oneness (التهليل) has already preceded in the commentary on His saying: وَإِلَٰهُكُمْ إِلَٰهٌ وَاحِدٌ لَّا إِلَٰهَ إِلَّا هُوَ "Your God is the one God; there is no god except Him" (2:163).

His saying: عَالِمُ الْغَيْبِ وَالشَّهَادَةِ "Knower of the Unseen and the Sensible." The Sensible (الشهادة) is that which is witnessed and present before the perceiver, and the Unseen (الغيب) is its opposite. These are two relative meanings; it is possible for a thing to be "sensible" in relation to one thing and "unseen" in relation to another. The matter revolves around a type of encompassment (إحاطة) of the thing—whether through sense, imagination, intellect, or existence—which is "the sensible," and its absence, which is "the unseen." Every assumed unseen or sensible thing is, as such, encompassed by Him and known.
